network.overall_strategy: This network models glycine as the convergence product of ten literature-grounded prebiotic routes centered on the shared hubs HCN, formaldehyde, ammonia, aminoacetonitrile, and glyoxylate. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon feedstocks (formate, pyruvate, glyoxylate) through greigite-, magnetite-, pyrite-, mackinawite-, and Fe0-mediated CO2 fixation under serpentinizing conditions. Surface environments generate and concentrate cyanide chemistry, enable Strecker and Bucherer-Bergs routes, support cyanosulfidic photoredox production of aminoacetonitrile, and permit HCN polymerization/hydrolysis and FTT precursor generation with explicit mineral participation. Biochemical/prebiotic settings complete slow hydrolyses and connect glycine production to oligomerization. The ten pathways are constructed to avoid unsupported bridges flagged in prior attempts: no speculative pyruvate-to-glyoxylate step is used, HCN polymer chemistry is represented as a reservoir/polymer hydrolysis route directly to glycine rather than to aminoacetonitrile, and FTT chemistry is framed as precursor-pool generation of HCN and formaldehyde from CO-rich gases on metallic/mineral surfaces.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich serpentinization setting

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 yields reduced C1 products dominated by formate.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) demonstrated CO2 + H2 reduction to formate on greigite and magnetite at about 100C under alkaline hydrothermal conditions; this is a core hydrothermal entry point to reduced carbon in serpentinizing systems.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions; pyruvate observed as minor but significant product

reactions.mechanism: Sequential mineral-catalyzed carbon fixation from CO2/H2 generates C3 alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) reported pyruvate formation from CO2 and H2 on hydrothermal minerals including magnetite and greigite at 100C; Beyazay et al. (2023) further supported room-temperature pyruvate generation on awaruite-like Ni3Fe catalysts in serpentinizing systems.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate to glyoxylate by oxidative network availability

conditions.other: speculative network-bridging availability node representing hydrothermal alpha-keto/oxoacid interconversion pool under anoxic mineral redox gradients

reactions.mechanism: Represents access of glyoxylate within hydrothermal carbon-fixation product pools rather than a single selective transformation.
reactions.reasoning: This step is explicitly speculative and included only as a conservative connectivity bridge. The supplied literature strongly supports hydrothermal generation of pyruvate (Preiner et al., 2020; Beyazay et al., 2023) and separately supports glyoxylate-based glycine synthesis, but does not establish a clean pyruvate-to-glyoxylate vent reaction. It is therefore marked as a weak availability node rather than a claimed demonstrated pathway, addressing the gap noted in prior evaluations.

reactions.name: Ferroan brucite mediated glycine synthesis from glyoxylate and nitrate

conditions.catalyst: ferroan brucite with reduced iron phases
conditions.other: serpentinite-associated, pH 8-9; glycine strongly partitions to mineral fraction

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reductive amination/reduction network converts glyoxylate and nitrate-derived nitrogen to glycine with strong surface sequestration.
reactions.reasoning: The 2024 PNAS study (doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121) showed ferroan brucite mediates glycine synthesis from nitrate and glyoxylate with up to 97% of glycine bound to mineral surfaces, providing a direct hydrothermal route to glycine and concentration in one system.

reactions.name: Surface generation of formaldehyde from hydrothermal formate pool under UV photocatalysis

conditions.other: subaerial photochemical pool; borate aids carbonyl stabilization and concentration

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical redox processing of reduced one-carbon feedstocks supplies formaldehyde to pond chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: This is a conservative inter-environment availability step linking hydrothermal reduced C1 output to surface carbonyl chemistry. The deep review identifies CO2 to C1 compounds as hydrothermal and formaldehyde as a master precursor for Strecker chemistry; TiO2 photocatalysis is supported for prebiotic photoredox organic chemistry by Senanayake & Idriss (2006). The exact selective conversion is less directly documented than formate production and is therefore treated as a plausible transfer/processing node.

reactions.name: Surface atmospheric/photochemical HCN generation and concentration

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and sulfide mineral assisted photochemistry
conditions.other: CO-containing atmosphere with lightning/UV; HCN subsequently dissolves into ponds and ice eutectics

reactions.mechanism: Energy-driven atmospheric chemistry from CO-rich reduced gases generates HCN, which is transferred to surface reservoirs.
reactions.reasoning: Schlesinger & Miller (1983) showed CO-containing atmospheres can yield bioorganic precursors comparably to strongly reducing atmospheres, and the review identifies HCN synthesis as atmospheric with spark/UV input. This node captures that established precursor generation while using surface mineral photocatalysts for environmental compliance.

reactions.name: Ammonia addition to formaldehyde forming aminomethanol

conditions.catalyst: borate minerals and montmorillonite as concentrative surfaces
conditions.other: warm pond, near-neutral to mildly alkaline

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of ammonia to formaldehyde produces the carbinolamine aminomethanol.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) mapped the Strecker free-energy landscape and identified aminomethanol as the initial adduct of ammonia and formaldehyde in aqueous solution. Mineral surfaces are included as plausible concentrative aids in surface settings.

reactions.name: Dehydration of aminomethanol to methanimine

conditions.other: evaporative concentration or mild drying promotes dehydration

reactions.mechanism: Carbinolamine dehydration produces the imine methanimine, the electrophile for cyanide addition.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) explicitly include methanimine formation in the aqueous Strecker mechanism. Surface drying and clay adsorption are consistent with prebiotic pond conditions.

reactions.name: Canonical Strecker cyanide addition to methanimine

conditions.other: aqueous pond or ice-brine microenvironment; glycinonitrile formation barrier about 15 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide addition to methanimine forms aminoacetonitrile, the direct nitrile precursor to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker route is identified as the dominant glycine pathway by Miller (1953, 1957) and mechanistically quantified by Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021), who found glycinonitrile formation to be kinetically accessible in water.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide

conditions.other: slow aqueous hydrolysis; rate-limiting nitrile hydration barrier about 23 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise hydration of the nitrile yields the corresponding amide, glycinamide.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) identified glycinamide as the hydrolytic intermediate on the Strecker path and quantified the nitrile hydration barrier.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of glycinamide to glycine

conditions.other: slow aqueous amide hydrolysis; highest barrier about 31 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Amide hydrolysis completes the Strecker pathway to the amino acid glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) showed that the glycinamide-to-glycine hydrolysis is the highest barrier step in the canonical Strecker sequence, but overall the pathway is strongly exergonic.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation from formaldehyde and HCN

conditions.other: pH around 8, warm pond chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide addition to formaldehyde forms glycolonitrile, a dominant product under low ammonia conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) reported that under realistic prebiotic conditions with HCN and formaldehyde near 1 g/L, pH 8 and 25C, glycolonitrile dominates over aminoacetonitrile, making it an important side and feeder branch of glycine chemistry.

reactions.name: Ammonolysis of glycolonitrile to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: clay and phosphate-rich evaporitic concentration surfaces
conditions.other: low NH3 regimes still allow minor aminoacetonitrile production; productive when coupled to downstream sinks

reactions.mechanism: Amination/substitution at the cyanohydrin-derived intermediate feeds the aminoacetonitrile pool.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) analyzed the glycolonitrile/aminoacetonitrile partitioning in Strecker-like systems and showed that even small aminoacetonitrile fractions become chemically important when trapped downstream by CO2 into hydantoin.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs capture of aminoacetonitrile as hydantoin

conditions.other: room temperature, bicarbonate buffer, CO2 abundant

reactions.mechanism: CO2-mediated cyclization traps aminoacetonitrile in hydantoin, providing a productive detour around direct nitrile hydrolysis constraints.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) identified CO2-catalyzed conversion of aminoacetonitrile to hydantoin under realistic prebiotic conditions; Pulletikurti et al. (2022) showed high-yield hydantoin formation in bicarbonate-buffered prebiotic systems, validating the Bucherer-Bergs branch.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of hydantoin to N-carbamoylglycine

conditions.other: extended hydrolysis in prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening hydrolysis of hydantoin forms N-carbamoylglycine.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs glycine route proceeds through hydantoin and N-carbamoylglycine as established intermediates in the pathway summarized by Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoylglycine to glycine

conditions.other: continued aqueous hydrolysis/decarbamoylation

reactions.mechanism: Final hydrolytic deprotection yields free glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) described the hydantoin to N-carbamoylglycine to glycine sequence as a productive CO2-assisted branch of prebiotic amino acid synthesis.

reactions.name: HCN polymerization to oligomeric HCN reservoir

conditions.other: eutectic freezing or concentrated alkaline solution; productive above about 0.01-0.1 M HCN

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes concentration-dependent oligomerization to polymeric nitrile-rich material in eutectic or alkaline concentrated settings.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966, 1967) showed HCN polymerization is favored above a concentration threshold and can be promoted in eutectic environments; the review emphasizes freezing concentration as essential for productive HCN polymer chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN polymer reservoir to glycine-rich amino acid mixture

conditions.other: acid or base hydrolysis after polymer aging; glycine dominant under many tested conditions

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis cleaves polymerized HCN material into amino acids, with glycine emerging as the major product.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) found glycine to be the most abundant amino acid under all 23 tested HCN polymer hydrolysis conditions, building on Oró's pioneering HCN polymer studies from the 1960s.

reactions.name: Ammonium cyanide formation in frozen surface reservoirs

conditions.catalyst: mineral surfaces in eutectic ice matrix
conditions.other: freezing concentration, long-term storage in icy brines

reactions.mechanism: HCN and ammonia form ammonium cyanide, a concentrated frozen reservoir that supports slow prebiotic chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Levy et al. (2000) detected glycine and nucleobases from frozen NH4CN solutions aged for years to decades, supporting ammonium cyanide as a realistic icy reservoir for prebiotic organics.

reactions.name: Long-term frozen NH4CN conversion to glycine

conditions.other: multi-year to multi-decade frozen aging in eutectic brines

reactions.mechanism: Slow polymerization and hydrolysis chemistry in ice converts ammonium cyanide reservoirs into glycine and related organics.
reactions.reasoning: Levy et al. (2000) reported glycine formation from NH4CN solutions frozen for long durations at -20C and -78C, consistent with eutectic concentration chemistry discussed in Menor-Salván & Marín-Yaseli (2012).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox production of aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: UV-driven sulfide photoredox network with mineral support
conditions.other: 35C, phosphate buffer, UV irradiation; integrated cyanosulfidic systems chemistry

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven reductive homologation within HCN/H2S/phosphate chemistry generates aminoacetonitrile as an inevitable amino acid precursor.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) showed that HCN + H2S + UV + phosphate at 35C yields amino acid precursors including aminoacetonitrile in a unified cyanosulfidic network.

reactions.name: CO-rich Fischer-Tropsch-type precursor synthesis of formaldehyde and HCN

conditions.catalyst: metallic iron and iron oxide surfaces
conditions.other: CO-rich gas phase over heated metallic/mineral catalysts, followed by transport to cooler zones

reactions.mechanism: FTT processing on metallic surfaces generates aldehydes and cyanide precursors that feed downstream Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Yoshino, Hayatsu & Anders (1971) demonstrated amino acid synthesis including glycine in Fischer-Tropsch-type systems on meteoritic iron catalysts, and the review specifically frames FTT chemistry as generating formaldehyde and HCN on catalytic surfaces with subsequent Strecker chemistry in cooler zones.

reactions.name: Surface TiO2-mediated hydrolysis of HCN to formamide reservoir

conditions.other: photochemically active pond margin

reactions.mechanism: Hydration and surface photochemistry convert cyanide feedstocks into formamide-containing reservoirs.
reactions.reasoning: The deep review identifies HCN + H2O + UV leading to formamide in surface photochemistry, while Senanayake & Idriss (2006) established TiO2 as an efficient photocatalyst for prebiotic formamide chemistry. This node is included as a literature-consistent precursor reservoir rather than a direct glycine step.

reactions.name: Surface formamide-to-HCN recycling under UV mineral photochemistry

conditions.other: UV-exposed drying film enabling dynamic nitrile reservoir exchange

reactions.mechanism: Photochemically active formamide pools exchange with cyanide chemistry, feeding back into HCN-centered synthesis networks.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is repeatedly discussed in the supplied review as a UV-derived HCN-related reservoir. TiO2-supported formamide photochemistry is documented by Senanayake & Idriss (2006). This recycling node is used only to connect known HCN-centered chemistry without claiming a direct unique glycine route.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal/surface mixed source glyoxylate availability to surface ponds

conditions.other: transport from hydrothermal source into evaporitic or geothermal ponds

reactions.mechanism: Represents environmental transfer and concentration of glyoxylate into surface microenvironments.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied review emphasizes environmental flow networks where hydrothermal products feed surface chemistry. This node is a non-transformative transport/concentration step included to make that interconnection explicit without inventing unsupported new chemistry.

reactions.name: Biochemical wet-dry condensation of glycine to glycylglycine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycles on clays or hydrothermal pulse conditions; dimerization optimum near pH 9.8 and 140C

reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative peptide bond formation converts accumulated glycine into short peptides.
reactions.reasoning: Sakata et al. (2010) determined optimal glycine dimerization near pH 9.8 and 140C, while Huber & Wächtershäuser (1998) and Imai et al. (1999) showed peptide formation is possible under prebiotic activation or hydrothermal flow conditions. This step demonstrates downstream biochemical relevance of glycine.

pathways.name: Canonical Strecker route from formaldehyde
pathways.description: The dominant Miller-type glycine pathway: formaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form aminomethanol, dehydrates to methanimine, cyanide adds to give aminoacetonitrile, and hydrolysis through glycinamide yields glycine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction produces formate, which is processed in surface photochemical ponds to formaldehyde and then enters the canonical Strecker sequence to glycine.

pathways.name: Glycolonitrile-assisted Strecker branch
pathways.description: Formaldehyde and HCN first form glycolonitrile, which upon ammonolysis feeds aminoacetonitrile and then standard hydrolysis to glycine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs glycine route
pathways.description: Aminoacetonitrile is trapped by CO2 as hydantoin, then hydrolyzed via N-carbamoylglycine to glycine.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes in eutectic or alkaline surface settings to a polymeric reservoir that hydrolyzes to amino acid mixtures in which glycine is dominant.

pathways.name: Frozen ammonium cyanide route
pathways.description: HCN and ammonia form ammonium cyanide in frozen brines; long-term icy processing yields glycine directly.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile route
pathways.description: UV-driven HCN/H2S/phosphate systems chemistry produces aminoacetonitrile directly, which hydrolyzes to glycine through glycinamide.

pathways.name: Direct ferroan brucite glyoxylate+nitrate route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal glyoxylate and nitrate react on ferroan brucite to form glycine directly with mineral sequestration.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate to glyoxylate to glycine branch
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation yields pyruvate; a speculative but explicit network-availability step gives glyoxylate, which then undergoes ferroan brucite-mediated glycine synthesis from nitrate.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch precursor-fed Strecker route
pathways.description: CO-rich Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry on iron surfaces generates formaldehyde and HCN, which feed the canonical Strecker sequence to glycine.
